 {"id":"bd-1cj","title":"[EPIC] WAL + crash recovery (formal crash model)","description":"## Context
WAL and crash recovery are the correctness foundation. With MVCC and concurrent writers, we must be explicit about:
- what constitutes a committed transaction on disk
- what crash models we handle (torn writes, reordering, metadata durability)
- how recovery reconstructs a consistent state

## Goal
Implement a crash model and a WAL/recovery design that is both:
- compatible with SQLite file formats at rest
- correct under the defined crash+fsync assumptions

## Non-Negotiables
- Crash model written down as a precise contract (not prose-only).
- Recovery correctness is validated by tests that inject partial writes/corruption.
- MVCC and WAL interaction is specified (visibility vs durability).

## Deliverables
- Formal crash model spec
- WAL frame format + WAL index format (or compatibility story)
- Checkpoint algorithm + invariants
- Recovery algorithm + tests (kill -9 simulation, torn-write simulation)

## Success Criteria
- For the declared crash model, we can prove and test: after restart, DB is consistent and reflects exactly the set of committed transactions.
- Recovery is deterministic and idempotent.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-07T04:53:05.949443684Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T04:53:05.949443684Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["crash-recovery","durability","wal"]}
 {"id":"bd-1d2","title":"Implement MVCC-aware WAL frame format","description":"## Context

Implement the WAL frame format and commit semantics for our engine:
- define what constitutes a committed transaction on disk
- encode the data needed for recovery and checkpoint

## Acceptance Criteria
- Format and commit rules are consistent with `bd-1ud` (crash model).
- Round-trip tests: write frames and parse them back.
- Recovery can distinguish committed vs uncommitted records.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T04:42:17.294442003Z","updated_at":"2026-02-07T05:01:38.139931473Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1d2","depends_on_id":"bd-1cj","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T04:54:55.097797214Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1d2","depends_on_id":"bd-1ud","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T04:43:03.047145187Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1d2","depends_on_id":"bd-ya9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T04:43:02.969614296Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1it","title":"[EPIC] MVCC-native storage engine","description":"## Context
This project’s primary differentiator is concurrent writers. If MVCC is bolted on later, we will bake single-writer assumptions into pager/cache/WAL APIs and rewrite them.

## Goal
Implement the storage foundation as MVCC-native from day one: snapshot-aware page reads, page-level version chains, eager conflict detection, bounded version growth via GC, and a cache architecture that is policy-pluggable.

## Non-Negotiables
- Safe Rust only (workspace forbids unsafe code).
- Pager APIs are snapshot/txn-aware (no hidden globals).
- Conflict detection is fail-fast (Busy), not wait-based.

## Deliverables
- MVCC core types (TxnId, Snapshot, Version metadata)
- Visibility engine + conflict detection
- MVCC-aware pager interface
- Version GC with formal reclaimability predicate
- Cache policy abstraction (so ARC/TinyLFU/etc can be evaluated, not hard-coded)

## Success Criteria
- Concurrent writers modifying disjoint pages can commit without blocking each other.
- Version chain length is bounded under sustained load (GC keeps memory stable).
- Property tests cover key invariants (visibility, no lost updates, deadlock freedom-by-construction).","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-07T04:52:43.084679120Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T04:52:56.429362309Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mvcc","pager","storage"]}

 ## 2. Why Page-Level MVCC
 
 ### 2.1 The Problem
 
-In WAL mode, C SQLite allows unlimited concurrent readers but only ONE writer
-at a time. The `WAL_WRITE_LOCK` (byte 120 of the WAL index shared memory)
-is an exclusive advisory lock. Any connection attempting to write while another
-holds this lock receives `SQLITE_BUSY`.
+In WAL mode, C SQLite allows multiple concurrent readers but caps the number
+of simultaneously active reader locks via `WAL_NREADER` in the wal-index shared
+memory (default: 5). It still allows only ONE writer at a time. The
+`WAL_WRITE_LOCK` (byte 120 of the WAL index shared memory) is an exclusive
+advisory lock. Any connection attempting to write while another holds this lock
+receives `SQLITE_BUSY` (or `SQLITE_BUSY_SNAPSHOT` in the fork-protection case).
 
 For applications with mixed read/write workloads across different tables or
 different regions of the same table, this is a needless bottleneck. Two users
